title: Building Inspection Services

alternatives.name: Residential transaction and new-home inspection providers
inclusion_criteria: Private firms classified in NAICS 541350 whose largest revenue family is general home inspection, new-home construction inspection, or combined home-and-pest inspection.
The inspected property is primarily a house, condominium, townhome, or other dwelling, and the service ordinarily evaluates multiple building systems for a buyer, seller, owner, or builder.
Firms may be independent, multi-inspector, enterprise, franchised, or multi-location; organizational form does not change the service-family classification.
When two enumerated service families are tied for the largest revenue share, assign the firm to the lexicographically first stable archetype ID.
exclusion_criteria: Firms whose largest revenue family is commercial building inspection.
Firms whose largest revenue family is a specific structural, electrical, roofing, HVAC, septic, exterior, moisture, recreational-facility, or other building-element inspection.

Government establishments that conduct inspections and enforce building codes, pest-control firms, environmental consulting firms, real-estate appraisers, and construction-materials testing laboratories.
alternatives.rationale: The 2022 Economic Census reports home inspection at 28.3%, new-home construction inspection at 11.7%, and combined home-and-pest inspection at 4.4% of NAICS 541350 receipts, totaling 44.4%. Applying that receipts share to the derived 40-firm band gives 17.76, rounded to 18. The range is deliberately wide because product receipts are establishment-level, firms may report more than one product, and the target band consists only of unusually large firms. The business-structure source confirms that residential inspection operators span solo, multi-inspector, and enterprise segments but does not measure the target band.

alternatives.name: Commercial general-building inspection providers
inclusion_criteria: Private firms classified in NAICS 541350 whose largest revenue family is commercial building inspection.

The service evaluates multiple systems or the overall physical condition of commercial, industrial, institutional, or multifamily property for owners, buyers, lenders, investors, or property managers.
When two enumerated service families are tied for the largest revenue share, assign the firm to the lexicographically first stable archetype ID.
exclusion_criteria: Firms whose largest revenue family is residential general or new-home inspection.
Firms whose largest revenue family is a specific building-element inspection.
Engineering-design firms, environmental consultants, public building-code enforcement bodies, and construction-materials testing laboratories whose primary activity falls outside NAICS 541350.
alternatives.rationale: Commercial building inspection represents 24.9% of 2022 NAICS 541350 receipts. Applying that share to 40 firms gives 9.96, rounded to 10. Its upper range is widened substantially because commercial providers may be disproportionately represented among firms with 100 or more employees, the only derived employment-size classes placed in the target band.

alternatives.name: Specific building-element inspection providers
inclusion_criteria: Private firms classified in NAICS 541350 whose largest revenue family is inspection of a specific structural, electrical, roofing, HVAC, septic, exterior, moisture, recreational-facility, or other building element.
Inspection and condition reporting, rather than repair, engineering design, environmental consulting, pest control, or laboratory testing, is the primary activity.
When two enumerated service families are tied for the largest revenue share, assign the firm to the lexicographically first stable archetype ID.
exclusion_criteria: Firms whose largest revenue family is residential general or new-home inspection.
Firms whose largest revenue family is commercial general-building inspection.
Environmental hazard detection, pest inspection, construction-materials testing, and government code-enforcement establishments.
alternatives.rationale: Specific-element inspection represents 17.5% of 2022 NAICS 541350 receipts. Applying that share to 40 firms gives 7.0. The range allows for concentration in regulated specialties and for ambiguity between a multi-system commercial inspection and several element-specific inspections.

residual.name: Mixed, other-related, and unclassified inspection providers
residual.rationale: Residual firms are those whose largest revenue family is other services related to building inspection or another non-enumerated product, plus firms whose product mix cannot be classified from available evidence. The base is the remainder after the three receipt-share-derived archetype counts: 40 minus 18 minus 10 minus 7 equals 5.

sources.title: 2022 Economic Census Selected Sectors: Industry by Products for the U.S. and States
2022 Economic Census Building Inspection Services Survey Instrument

target_archetype.selection_basis: The derived dataset estimates 40 firms in the $1-10M EBITDA band. The 2022 Economic Census product-receipt shares are used as a disclosed population proxy to allocate those firms before any economic research: residential-related 44.4% x 40 = 17.76 (base 18), commercial general 24.9% x 40 = 9.96 (base 10), and specific-element 17.5% x 40 = 7.0 (base 7), leaving a base residual of 5. Residential general inspection has the largest base count and is selected. This is not a direct target-band firm census.

value_basis.scope: Cash labor and independent-contractor cost structure of a target-band residential transaction and new-home inspection provider, allocated by mutually exclusive primary work function.

labor_contractor_cash_cost_share.rationale: No contemporaneous target-archetype cost accounting was found. This frozen class-neutral range is a bounded structure for a field-service business with paid or contracted inspectors and centralized support; it is not an empirical estimate.
caveats: The derived 541350 labor share and 5413 occupational mix are broader-industry measures and are not substituted for target-specific weights.
Owner compensation, inspector classification, benefits, subcontracting, travel reimbursement, and franchise fees can materially change the cash-cost share.
The range is intentionally broad and must not be narrowed without target-archetype evidence.

roles.title: Onsite inspection and evidence capture
roles.rationale: Assumed largest cash-cost function: travel to properties, physical examination of structures and systems, measurements, photographs, and field notes.

roles.title: Report drafting and delivery
roles.rationale: Assumed allocation for organizing field evidence, describing deficiencies, assembling the inspection report, and delivering findings.

roles.title: Scheduling and client coordination
roles.rationale: Assumed allocation for intake, quoting, appointment routing, access coordination, reminders, and client communications not included in report delivery.

roles.title: Quality review and technical supervision
roles.rationale: Assumed allocation for report review, technical escalation, standards adherence, training, and inspector supervision.

roles.title: Sales and general administration
roles.rationale: Assumed allocation for referral development, local marketing, billing, bookkeeping, compliance administration, and general management support.

value_basis.rationale: The available dataset supplies a six-digit labor-share estimate but only a four-digit occupational mix dominated by engineering and architecture roles that is not a valid deterministic proxy for the selected residential archetype. An explicit assumption basis preserves a usable, mutually exclusive functional role structure without presenting unsupported target-specific precision.
caveats: All cash-cost values and role weights in this basis are assumptions with no source claim.
Weights sum to 1.00 and allocate people who perform multiple functions according to primary cash-cost function rather than job title.
The basis excludes nonlabor vehicle, equipment, insurance, software, franchise, occupancy, and other operating costs.
Selection ranges overlap the commercial-general archetype, whose staffing and contractor structure may differ materially.

source_hints.source: Residential inspection software vendors, enterprise inspection operators, state licensing bodies, and practitioner interviews
source_hints.why: Establish the current intake-to-report workflow, systems in use, deployment constraints, training burden, quality controls, and adoption schedule for the selected archetype.

source_hints.source: Operator price books and invoices, franchise disclosure documents, real-estate partner agreements, customer surveys, and transaction-volume data
source_hints.why: Measure whether workflow changes affect inspection prices, ancillary-service attachment, referral relationships, throughput, or customer demand over time.

source_hints.source: Business brokers, lower-middle-market transaction databases, franchise resale records, and disclosed acquisitions of residential inspection providers
source_hints.why: Estimate the number of target-archetype firms actually offered for sale and the relationship between listings, closed deals, and the estimated band population.

source_hints.source: Closed transaction records, broker market reports, lender materials, and buyer underwriting for multi-inspector residential inspection firms
source_hints.why: Anchor entry and downside exit multiples to the selected archetype and size band while distinguishing advertised asking multiples from closed transactions.

source_hints.source: State licensing rules, inspection standards, insurer requirements, franchise operating manuals, customer contracts, and large-operator organization charts
source_hints.why: Identify which licensed judgment, onsite examination, report certification, relationship, escalation, and management responsibilities must remain with the operating company after workflow changes.

implementation: What are the current steps, systems, labor hours, error controls, and handoffs from lead intake through onsite inspection, report delivery, billing, and follow-up?
Which workflow changes have target-archetype operators actually deployed, and what are the documented adoption, training, integration, supervision, and quality-assurance schedules over five years?
How do licensing, standards of practice, insurance, data custody, and client-contract requirements constrain implementation?
commercial_retention: How are base inspections and ancillary services priced, and what evidence links workflow changes to price, volume, attach rate, turnaround time, or referral retention?
How concentrated are referrals among agents, builders, lenders, and direct consumers, and what limits the provider's ability to retain operating gains?
What five-year retention range is supported after accounting for price competition, transaction cyclicality, franchise fees, and customer acquisition costs?
sale_availability: How many U.S. residential general-inspection firms in the target band were marketed and sold in recent years?
What bridge converts broker listings or disclosed deals into an annual sale-availability share of the estimated 18-firm base population?
How should franchise resales, asset sales, tuck-ins, mixed-service firms, and undisclosed transactions be treated?
valuation_robustness: What closed-transaction entry multiples are supported for multi-inspector or enterprise residential inspection providers in the target band?
What downside exit multiple is supported under weaker housing turnover, referral disruption, owner departure, or margin normalization?
How do recurring ancillary services, franchise status, customer concentration, geographic concentration, owner dependence, and inspector classification affect comparability?
operator_survival: Which onsite, licensed, judgment-intensive, certification, client-facing, and supervisory duties must remain inside the operating company in year five?
What staffing, credential, insurance, escalation, and quality-review structure is required to perform those duties safely and legally?
Can the surviving structure sustain inspection quality, client accountability, referral relationships, and exception handling across seasonal demand?

special_notes: This specification describes only the selected residential-general-inspection archetype, not NAICS 541350 as a whole.
Selection uncertainty is true because the residential and commercial target-band count ranges overlap; all later work must retain the selected residential record and the widened ranges.

The archetype allocation uses industry receipt shares as a proxy for target-band firm shares. Every later use must preserve the explicit size and establishment-to-firm bridge and may not describe the counts as observed firms.
The assumption value basis has no evidentiary sources. Its cash-cost range and exact role membership are frozen and must not be replaced by the broad 5413 occupational mix.

Public code-enforcement bodies, environmental consulting, pest control, real-estate appraisal, engineering design, and construction-materials testing remain outside the target boundary even when they perform adjacent inspection work.
